title: Inkling: Reading the Model Card Across Checkpoints and Claims

central_claim.text: Inkling is a 66-layer sparse mixture-of-experts multimodal Transformer whose architecture, access options, checkpoints, and evaluation claims must be kept separate to understand what the release evidence actually supports.

tldr: Inkling is a 66-layer decoder-only sparse mixture-of-experts model with 975 billion total parameters and 41 billion active parameters per token path.
It accepts text, images, and audio, produces text, and advertises up to a one-million-token context window, while Tinker exposes smaller 64K and 256K options.
The release reports broad benchmark coverage and adjustable reasoning effort, but some results use internal harnesses or a checkpoint different from the released weights.

Open weights do not mean consumer-scale deployment: the mutable model card retrieved on 2026-07-18 listed 2 TB of aggregate VRAM for BF16 and 600 GB for NVFP4, while benchmark parity between those checkpoints is not established.

blocks.question: Why did Thinking Machines Lab release Inkling?
paragraphs: The release frames Inkling as a broad base model for customization rather than a model optimized to lead every benchmark. Its intended uses include agentic systems, coding assistants, chatbots, retrieval-augmented generation, instruction following, and multimodal applications.

That positioning matters because the provider explicitly says Inkling is not the strongest model overall. The proposed value is the combination of open weights, multimodal input, controllable reasoning effort, and availability for fine-tuning.

blocks.question: What is materially different about the release?
paragraphs: Inkling combines a large sparse model with native text, image, and audio input and makes the weights available in original and quantized forms. Only a subset of the 975 billion parameters is active for a token, which separates total model capacity from the 41-billion-parameter active path.

The release also exposes an effort control intended to trade generated tokens for performance. This is a product and training claim about controllability, not proof that Inkling dominates models at every effort level or task.

blocks.question: How does Inkling process a token and multiple input modalities?
paragraphs: Inkling is a decoder-only autoregressive Transformer with 66 layers. Its feed-forward backbone is a sparse mixture of experts: each token is routed to 6 of 256 routed experts, while 2 shared experts are always active. The selected and shared expert outputs are combined before the token continues through the network.

The release says local and global attention layers are interleaved at a 5-to-1 ratio with 8 key-value heads. Images and audio are converted into representations, projected into the same hidden space as text, and processed jointly by the decoder. The release describes 40-by-40 image patches through a four-layer hierarchical MLP and dMel spectrograms for audio; the website model card describes the same path more generally as a hierarchical patch encoder and discrete audio-token encoding.

The provider reports pretraining on 45 trillion tokens across text, images, audio, and video, followed by synthetic supervised fine-tuning and large-scale reinforcement learning. These are provider release claims; the model card discloses only broad data categories and provenance rather than a reproducible dataset inventory.

blocks.question: What would a local deployment decision look like?
paragraphs: Start by choosing a checkpoint, not by reading the phrase open weights as a hardware promise. In the content-addressed model-card retrieval from 2026-07-18, the BF16 checkpoint requires at least 2 TB of aggregate VRAM; the listed examples are 8 NVIDIA B300 GPUs or 16 H200 GPUs. The same snapshot gives a 600 GB minimum for NVFP4: W4A4 can use 4 B300 GPUs but requires SM100-or-newer architecture, while W4A16 can use 8 H200 GPUs. The live page remains mutable, so the retrieval hash in the source record must accompany these figures.

The quantized option reduces memory requirements, but the release does not identify the precision behind the main benchmark table or provide a BF16-versus-NVFP4 quality comparison. A deployment plan therefore cannot assume that the published benchmark values transfer unchanged to the quantized checkpoint.

blocks.question: What evidence does the release provide?
paragraphs: In the content-addressed provider-page retrievals from 2026-07-18, the main benchmark table reports Inkling at effort 0.99. Selected values include 29.7 percent on Humanity's Last Exam without tools, 46.0 percent with tools, 77.6 percent on SWE-bench Verified, 79.8 percent on IFBench, 73.5 percent on MMMU Pro Standard 10, 91.4 percent on VoiceBench, and 98.6 percent on StrongREJECT. These are vendor-published results across different benchmark families, not one common measurement scale; later live-page changes would require a new source hash.

The release states that the benchmark runs use temperature 1.0 and that coding evaluations allow trajectories up to 256K tokens. Some comparisons use externally reported scores; otherwise the provider uses internal harnesses. Inkling's Terminal Bench score uses an internal coding harness, contaminated web-search solutions were assigned zero, and VoiceBench required a formatting instruction because its grader is sensitive to output format.

The effort-sweep chart supports the narrower provider interpretation that performance can be traded against generated tokens. Its Humanity's Last Exam points came from an earlier checkpoint, and the release separately says its forecasting results came from testing on a checkpoint different from the released one.

blocks.question: Where should the claims be narrowed?
paragraphs: The provider says Inkling can hallucinate, fail to follow instructions, degrade in long multi-turn conversations, reproduce demographic or cultural biases, and perform unevenly across languages and subject domains. It recommends independent evaluation, application-level safeguards, and human oversight for high-stakes uses.

The one-million-token statement describes supported capacity, not demonstrated accuracy throughout that window. Tinker currently exposes 64K and 256K context options. The release also does not establish that the NVFP4 checkpoint matches BF16 benchmark quality or that base-checkpoint safety behavior survives downstream fine-tuning.

Training disclosure remains high level. The sources give broad categories and a 45-trillion-token release claim but not the dataset composition, rights breakdown, contamination analysis, language distribution, or training cutoff needed for a complete independent audit.

The model card, release page, and Acceptable Use Policy remain mutable live pages, but the exact official HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18 is content-addressed with SHA-256 and byte counts in the source records. Claims here describe those fixed retrievals; later page changes are not silently incorporated and require new hashes and review.

blocks.question: What should a careful reader conclude?
paragraphs: The release supports a strong descriptive conclusion: Inkling is a very large sparse multimodal model with downloadable checkpoints and broad vendor-reported evaluation coverage. It does not support calling the model best overall, consumer-deployable, fully open source, or proven at full quality across a one-million-token context.

Source boundaries are part of the evidence. The content-addressed website snapshot and initial checkpoint cards disagree on a few values, including GDPVal-AA and MMMU Pro. The exact model-card, release-page, and Acceptable Use Policy HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18 is identified by SHA-256 and byte count, while BF16 and NVFP4 are pinned to their initial release commits. The live pages remain mutable, but the source versions used here are reproducible.

The provider labels the model Apache 2.0, but its linked Model Acceptable Use Policy says that accessing or using the model materials is also conditioned on that policy. This explainer records both statements rather than treating open weights as a synonym for unrestricted use.

claims.text: In content-addressed official HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18, Thinking Machines Lab dates the initial Inkling release to July 15, 2026, labels the model Apache 2.0, and links an Acceptable Use Policy that says it also conditions use of the model materials.
Inkling is a 66-layer decoder-only sparse mixture-of-experts Transformer with 975 billion total parameters, 41 billion active parameters, 6 of 256 routed experts per token, and 2 shared experts.

The pinned initial BF16 card verifies text, image, and audio inputs with text output; the content-addressed provider pages retrieved on 2026-07-18 advertise support for a context window up to one million tokens.

The content-addressed model card retrieved on 2026-07-18 lists a minimum of 2 TB aggregate VRAM for BF16 and 600 GB for NVFP4, with distinct GPU and numeric-mode requirements; the checkpoint identities are pinned to their initial release commits.

In content-addressed pages retrieved on 2026-07-18, the provider positions Inkling as a broad customizable open-weights base with multimodal input and controllable effort, while explicitly stating that it is not the strongest model overall.

The content-addressed release page retrieved on 2026-07-18 reports pretraining on 45 trillion multimodal tokens and more than 30 million asynchronous reinforcement-learning rollouts after an initial synthetic supervised-fine-tuning stage.

In the content-addressed provider table retrieved on 2026-07-18, effort 0.99 results are 29.7 percent on Humanity's Last Exam without tools, 77.6 percent on SWE-bench Verified, 79.8 percent on IFBench, 73.5 percent on MMMU Pro Standard 10, 91.4 percent on VoiceBench, and 98.6 percent on StrongREJECT.

The content-addressed release page retrieved on 2026-07-18 interprets its effort sweep as evidence that Inkling can exchange generated tokens for performance and says it matches Nemotron 3 Ultra on Terminal Bench at roughly one-third of the generated tokens.

The content-addressed release page retrieved on 2026-07-18 says the forecasting results came from a checkpoint different from the released one and that the effort chart's Humanity's Last Exam points came from an earlier checkpoint.

The official checkpoint card warns about hallucinations, instruction-following failures, degraded long-conversation performance, uneven language or domain performance, and the need for independent evaluation and human oversight.

The provider's content-addressed website snapshot retrieved on 2026-07-18 and the immutable initial checkpoint cards disagree on some published values, including GDPVal-AA and MMMU Pro; the fixed source identities establish the disagreement but do not explain which values the provider intends as canonical.

The sources do not establish that NVFP4 preserves BF16 benchmark quality, that useful accuracy holds throughout a one-million-token prompt, or that base-model safety transfers unchanged to fine-tuned checkpoints.

visuals.title: Stored capacity and one active token path are different quantities
visuals.question: How do 975B stored parameters relate to 41B active parameters?

items.label: Stored sparse model
items.detail: The downloadable model contains all routed and shared expert parameters.

items.label: Per-token routed experts
items.detail: The router selects 6 of 256 routed experts for one token.

items.label: Per-token shared experts
items.detail: Two shared experts are always included in that token's feed-forward path.

items.label: Combined active path
items.detail: Selected routed and shared experts together produce the reported active parameter count.

items.label: Deployment boundary
items.detail: Sparse activation lowers computation per token, but checkpoint storage still represents the much larger model.

visuals.interpretation: The 41B active path is a per-token computation property, not the stored checkpoint size.
limitations: Parameter counts do not by themselves establish latency, throughput, memory bandwidth, or benchmark quality.
visuals.alt_text: Stored capacity and one active token path are different quantities. Stored sparse model: 975B total; Per-token routed experts: 6 of 256; Per-token shared experts: 2 shared; Combined active path: 41B active; Deployment boundary.
visuals.fallback: The 41B active path is a per-token computation property, not the stored checkpoint size. Stored sparse model: 975B total; Per-token routed experts: 6 of 256; Per-token shared experts: 2 shared; Combined active path: 41B active; Deployment boundary.

visuals.title: Routed and shared experts converge into one active token path
visuals.question: Which expert outputs are combined before the token continues?

items.label: Token representation
items.detail: One decoder token enters the sparse feed-forward layer.

items.label: Router
items.detail: The router scores the 256 routed experts for this token.

items.label: Selected routed outputs
items.detail: Only 6 of 256 routed experts process the token.

items.label: Always-active shared outputs
items.detail: Two shared experts process the same token independently of router selection.

items.label: Weighted expert merge
items.detail: The selected routed outputs and both shared outputs are combined.

items.label: Active token path
items.detail: The merged sparse feed-forward result contributes to the reported 41B active parameters before the token continues.

visuals.interpretation: The active path is the convergence of routed and shared expert outputs—not a choice between the two branches.
limitations: The diagram does not expose undisclosed router weights or per-expert parameter counts.
visuals.alt_text: Routed and shared experts converge into one active token path. Token representation; Router; Selected routed outputs: 6 of 256; Always-active shared outputs: 2 shared; Weighted expert merge; Active token path: 41B active.
visuals.fallback: The active path is the convergence of routed and shared expert outputs—not a choice between the two branches. Token representation; Router; Selected routed outputs: 6 of 256; Always-active shared outputs: 2 shared; Weighted expert merge; Active token path: 41B active.

visuals.title: Text, image, and audio front ends converge before decoding
visuals.question: How do three modalities enter one autoregressive decoder?

items.label: Text tokens
items.detail: Token representations begin in the decoder hidden space.

items.label: Image patches
items.detail: 40×40 patches pass through a four-layer hierarchical MLP and projection.

items.label: Audio representation
items.detail: The release names dMel spectrograms; the website gives the broader discrete-audio-token description.

items.label: Shared hidden space
items.detail: Projected text, image, and audio representations are processed jointly.

items.label: Interleaved attention
items.detail: Local and global layers use the reported 5:1 ratio with eight key-value heads.

items.label: 66-layer autoregressive decoder
items.detail: The unified representation continues through the decoder and emits text.

visuals.interpretation: Distinct modality front ends project into a shared hidden representation consumed by one decoder path.
limitations: The two official source surfaces describe the audio path at different levels of specificity; the figure preserves both descriptions.
visuals.alt_text: Text, image, and audio front ends converge before decoding. Text tokens; Image patches: 40×40 · 4 layers; Audio representation; Shared hidden space; Interleaved attention: 5:1 · 8 KV heads; 66-layer autoregressive decoder: 66 layers.
visuals.fallback: Distinct modality front ends project into a shared hidden representation consumed by one decoder path. Text tokens; Image patches: 40×40 · 4 layers; Audio representation; Shared hidden space; Interleaved attention: 5:1 · 8 KV heads; 66-layer autoregressive decoder: 66 layers.

visuals.title: Provider-reported training proceeds through three broad stages
visuals.question: What training sequence is disclosed, and what remains undisclosed?

items.label: Multimodal pretraining
items.detail: Provider reports text, image, audio, and video data at very large scale.

items.label: Synthetic supervised fine-tuning
items.detail: Synthetic instruction data follows pretraining.

items.label: Large-scale reinforcement learning
items.detail: A reinforcement-learning stage follows synthetic SFT.

items.label: Released checkpoints
items.detail: BF16 and NVFP4 checkpoint forms are made available after the reported training sequence.

items.label: Disclosure boundary
items.detail: Sources give broad categories and provenance, not a reproducible dataset inventory, rights breakdown, contamination audit, language distribution, or cutoff.

visuals.interpretation: The release discloses a stage order and a 45T-token claim, while leaving the underlying dataset composition non-reproducible.
limitations: These are provider release claims rather than independently reproduced training records.
visuals.alt_text: Provider-reported training proceeds through three broad stages. Multimodal pretraining: 45T tokens; Synthetic supervised fine-tuning; Large-scale reinforcement learning; Released checkpoints; Disclosure boundary.
visuals.fallback: The release discloses a stage order and a 45T-token claim, while leaving the underlying dataset composition non-reproducible. Multimodal pretraining: 45T tokens; Synthetic supervised fine-tuning; Large-scale reinforcement learning; Released checkpoints; Disclosure boundary.

visuals.title: Checkpoint precision changes the deployment plan
visuals.question: Which memory, GPU, and architecture conditions belong to each checkpoint path?

items.label: BF16 checkpoint
items.detail: At least 2 TB aggregate VRAM; examples are 8 NVIDIA B300 or 16 H200 GPUs; pinned initial release commit.

items.label: NVFP4 · W4A4
items.detail: 600 GB aggregate minimum; 4 B300 GPUs; requires SM100-or-newer architecture.

items.label: NVFP4 · W4A16
items.detail: 600 GB aggregate minimum; 8 H200 GPUs; same pinned NVFP4 checkpoint.

items.label: Quality-transfer boundary
items.detail: The main benchmark table does not identify evaluated precision and no BF16-versus-NVFP4 quality comparison is provided.

visuals.interpretation: Checkpoint choice determines hardware conditions, while published benchmark quality cannot be assumed to transfer between precisions.
limitations: The content-addressed retrieval records provider requirements as of 2026-07-18; live pages can change.
visuals.alt_text: Checkpoint precision changes the deployment plan. BF16 checkpoint: ≥2 TB · 8 B300 or 16 H200; NVFP4 · W4A4: ≥600 GB · 4 B300 · SM100+; NVFP4 · W4A16: ≥600 GB · 8 H200; Quality-transfer boundary.
visuals.fallback: Checkpoint choice determines hardware conditions, while published benchmark quality cannot be assumed to transfer between precisions. BF16 checkpoint: ≥2 TB · 8 B300 or 16 H200; NVFP4 · W4A4: ≥600 GB · 4 B300 · SM100+; NVFP4 · W4A16: ≥600 GB · 8 H200; Quality-transfer boundary.

visuals.title: Some evaluation claims use non-release checkpoints
visuals.question: Which result surface belongs to which checkpoint boundary?

items.label: Effort-sweep chart
items.detail: Supports a performance-versus-generated-tokens trade-off across effort settings.

items.label: HLE effort-sweep points
items.detail: The release states these Humanity's Last Exam points came from an earlier checkpoint.

items.label: Forecasting results
items.detail: The release states forecasting used a checkpoint different from the released one.

items.label: Released checkpoints
items.detail: BF16 and NVFP4 are separately pinned releases; results from other checkpoints cannot silently transfer to them.

visuals.interpretation: The effort and forecasting claims are source-backed, but their checkpoint identities limit what can be inferred about released weights.
limitations: The cited sources do not provide a full checkpoint-by-checkpoint replication matrix.
visuals.alt_text: Some evaluation claims use non-release checkpoints. Effort-sweep chart: Effort sweep; HLE effort-sweep points: Earlier checkpoint; Forecasting results: Different checkpoint; Released checkpoints: BF16 + NVFP4.
visuals.fallback: The effort and forecasting claims are source-backed, but their checkpoint identities limit what can be inferred about released weights. Effort-sweep chart: Effort sweep; HLE effort-sweep points: Earlier checkpoint; Forecasting results: Different checkpoint; Released checkpoints: BF16 + NVFP4.

visuals.title: Supported context is not demonstrated accuracy
visuals.question: Which context lengths are advertised, hosted, or empirically unproven?

items.label: Provider capacity claim
items.detail: The content-addressed provider page advertises support up to one million tokens.

items.label: Tinker access options
items.detail: The cited hosted access surface exposes two smaller context choices.

items.label: Accuracy span
items.detail: The cited evidence does not establish useful accuracy across every position of a one-million-token prompt.

items.label: Precision and safety transfer
items.detail: The sources do not establish BF16/NVFP4 parity or unchanged safety after downstream fine-tuning.

visuals.interpretation: Capacity, hosted availability, and demonstrated accuracy are separate claims with different evidence.
limitations: This figure explains the cited paper's reported mechanism or evidence; it does not extend the claim beyond the stated conditions.
visuals.alt_text: Supported context is not demonstrated accuracy. Provider capacity claim: up to 1M; Tinker access options: 64K · 256K; Accuracy span; Precision and safety transfer.

visuals.title: Reproducibility depends on fixed source identities
visuals.question: Which mutable or immutable source supports each claim surface?

items.label: Provider website snapshot
items.detail: Model card, release page, and Acceptable Use Policy HTML retrieved 2026-07-18 are fixed by SHA-256 and byte count.

items.label: BF16 checkpoint card
items.detail: Initial release is pinned to its immutable repository commit.

items.label: NVFP4 checkpoint card
items.detail: Quantized release is pinned to a separate immutable initial commit.

items.label: GDPVal-AA and MMMU Pro
items.detail: The fixed website and initial checkpoint cards disagree on some values without naming one canonical set.

visuals.interpretation: Content addressing preserves what each official surface said and makes unresolved disagreements auditable.
limitations: Fixed identities preserve source conflict; they do not decide which value is authoritative.

glossary.definition: A model design that routes each token through a selected subset of feed-forward expert networks rather than activating every expert.
The parameters used along a token's routed computation path. This differs from the total parameters stored in all experts.
A specific saved set of model weights. Results from one checkpoint do not automatically describe another checkpoint or a later fine-tuned derivative.
The low-precision checkpoint format offered for Inkling to reduce aggregate VRAM requirements relative to BF16.
A control that changes how many generated reasoning tokens the model tends to use, allowing a trade between token use and measured performance.
The prompts, tools, execution environment, grading rules, and limits used to run an evaluation. Different harnesses can make nominally similar scores difficult to compare directly.

revision_notes: Initial review-required explainer fixture for the July 15, 2026 Inkling release.

paper_version v1 is a local Paper Atlas record for the initial release; the provider's mutable model card, release page, and Acceptable Use Policy are tied to exact official HTML retrieved on 2026-07-18.

Model card retrieval: SHA-256 fe653ffb5f4b9f54f011491f60cd8d6b9885d667484880d4566d76827f22a7e9, 65,631 bytes. Release page: SHA-256 cb28c6a6c8c47c68f55f2c636481bf35a1b9f5a349e5f00148c583fafbc138fc, 222,133 bytes.
Acceptable Use Policy retrieval: SHA-256 c62535263733dbeabb838ff881850928a878bc5c539ce1401a59a237bbf5c2e7, 25,968 bytes. Later live-page changes require a new content hash and review.

The BF16 repository is pinned to initial release commit 91b051f1ec836e6d56596c624c3775b495d797b1 and the NVFP4 repository to initial release commit 93a182fb0376affeaeecfa4658c37a0fe9e5fa9e; the mutable website card and immutable checkpoint cards contain small unresolved benchmark-value differences.
Apache 2.0 is recorded together with the provider's linked Acceptable Use Policy rather than presented as unrestricted use.
The visual is an original evidence matrix; no provider figure is reproduced.
Visual revision 3: paragraph-scoped figures now encode the manifest-selected topology, conditions, values, and evidence boundaries.
